key_departures.impact: Oweh had 7.5 sacks in 12 games for the Chargers in 2025 after a midseason trade from Baltimore, then posted 3 sacks and 2 forced fumbles in the Wild Card win. Signed a 4-year, $100M deal ($68M guaranteed) with Washington. Massive loss — he was the Chargers' most productive pass rusher and was only 27.

Johnson signed a 3-year, $49.5M contract with the Browns. The Chargers let their 2022 first-round guard walk after a season in which the interior line was historically bad. Departure further strips an already paper-thin guard room — only one of three 2025 interior starters now returns (Biadasz added at C, but both guard spots are essentially open).

Released March 4, saving nearly $10M in cap space. Becton's lone season in LA was plagued by injuries and inconsistent play. Release eliminates a starting RG spot, leaving both interior guard positions open entering free agency.
Starting center retired in February 2026. Created the most urgent IOL need heading into free agency — the Chargers had literally no interior starter on the roster before Biadasz was signed.
St-Juste had a breakout rotational year under Jesse Minter in 2025 despite being a bargain signing. His departure to Green Bay thins the CB depth but does not eliminate a starting-caliber player.
Signed a 1-year, $3M deal with Dallas. Ogbonnia played 9 games before an elbow injury ended his season. Was a rotational/depth piece; departure addressed by Tomlinson signing.
Signed a 1-year, $3M deal with Atlanta on March 13. Hand was an impactful rotational piece in 2025 but his departure was softened by the Tomlinson signing on the same day.
Released in a cost-cutting move saving ~$4.4M. Dissly's role had diminished to the point of healthy scratches. Left the Chargers with just one TE on the roster heading into free agency.
Conklin signed a 1-year deal with Detroit. Had only 7 catches for 101 yards in 13 games in 2025. Departure of a depth piece — team had already planned around replacing him.
Harris departed in free agency. He was the veteran complementary back behind Omarion Hampton. Departure addressed by Mitchell signing.
Salyer was viewed as a depth/scheme-misfit for new OC Mike McDaniel's system. His departure was not a significant roster blow but further thins OL depth.

signings.analysis: Extension signed January 26, well before free agency. Tart's 3-year, $30M deal with $20M guaranteed (with up to $37.5M in incentives) locks in the Chargers' best interior lineman through the window. His 12 run stops ranked 7th in the NFL in 2025. With Tart as the anchor, IDL is not a priority need — the Tomlinson addition further solidifies depth. Need fully addressed at the starting level.

Signed March 6 before the legal tampering window even opened. Biadasz (3yr/$30M, $17M guaranteed, $15M at signing) fills the vacant center spot vacated by Bozeman's retirement. A clear upgrade — Biadasz ranked 15th in PBWR vs. Bozeman's 30th. However, both guard spots remain wide open, so IOL is only partially addressed. Center need is resolved; guard need remains at high priority.

Re-signed March 7, fully guaranteed 1-year deal. Mack (35) remains the heart of the Chargers defense and a legitimate starter, but at age 35 coming off a 5.5-sack injury-shortened year, this is not a multi-year solution. With Oweh gone, the edge trio is now Mack (1-yr), Tuli Tuipulotu (final year of deal), and a significant hole at EDGE3. EDGE is still a meaningful draft need — the 1-year structure signals a bridge, not a solution.

Signed March 10. Strange brings familiarity with new OC Mike McDaniel's wide-zone system from their time together in Miami. The 2yr/$13M deal ($9M guaranteed) signals a compete-for-the-job contract, not a plug-and-play starter. Strange has experience at LG, C, and RG. His athleticism fits McDaniel's scheme, but this signing does not resolve the guard need on its own — one of the two guard spots still requires a starter-caliber addition via FA or the draft. IOL remains an elevated need despite Biadasz + Strange.

Re-signed March 11. Pipkins serves as a swing tackle and depth piece behind Rashawn Slater (LT) and Joe Alt (RT), both returning from season-ending injuries. His 2yr/$10M deal confirms a backup/depth role. OT is not a need given Slater and Alt's expected return — this is solid depth insurance at below-market cost.

Re-signed quietly during the legal tampering window on a 1-year deal (terms not fully disclosed; estimated based on market). Penning was acquired via trade midseason in 2025 and proved a serviceable depth piece. Primarily a camp/depth signing at OT — no need impact. IOL versatility adds some value.

Signed March 11. After releasing Will Dissly, the Chargers had one TE on the roster heading into FA. Kolar's 3yr/$24.3M deal with $17M guaranteed is a real investment — this is their guy. At 27, he spent his career behind Mark Andrews and Isaiah Likely in Baltimore, meaning his opportunity was always limited by depth chart. Pairs with Oronde Gadsden to form a balanced TE room (run-blocking Kolar + pass-catching Gadsden). TE need is fully addressed. GM Hortiz drafted Kolar in Baltimore, so this is a high-conviction move.

Signed after his release by Miami. Ingold is a Pro Bowl fullback who played four seasons under McDaniel with the Dolphins. His role in McDaniel's offense is significant — the FB position is a genuine chess piece in the wide-zone/playaction system, not just a blocker. Reduces the team's reliance on nose tackle Scott Matlock as a two-way player. No pre-existing FB need, but a genuine scheme-enhancing addition.

Signed March 12. Mitchell (2yr/$9.25M, $5M guaranteed) replaces Najee Harris as Omarion Hampton's primary complement. His 6.3 YPC career average and 4.37 speed give the Chargers a genuine big-play threat in McDaniel's wide-zone scheme. Pairs with Hampton and ERFA Kimani Vidal for a three-headed backfield. RB depth is now solid, though Hampton's injury history (9 games in 2025) means more depth could be welcome. Need partially to fully addressed.

Signed March 13. Tomlinson's 1yr/$7.5M deal ($6M guaranteed) is a depth/rotation signing to replace departing Da'Shawn Hand and Otito Ogbonnia. He had a poor 2025 season (PFF ranked him 114th of 127 IDL) but brings size (335 lbs), durability (142 career starts, 7 games missed in 9 years), and veteran rotational value. Slots behind Tart and Jamaree Caldwell. One-year structure means IDL depth remains something to monitor, but the starting picture is fine with Tart locked up.

Re-signed March 12 on a 1-year deal. Perryman returned as a veteran leader and rotational LB. Cap hit of just $2.1M reflects a depth/mentor role. LB was not a need heading in; this keeps the room intact with Daiyan Henley and the returning Perryman. 2026 third-rounder Junior Colson (missed 2025 with shoulder injury) will also compete for snaps.

Re-signed March 11. Phillips earned 2nd-Team All-Pro on special teams in 2025, leading the NFL with 24 tackles on special teams (a franchise record). His 2yr/$10.5M deal is well-earned and locks in one of the best special teams players in the league. Also contributes as a quality rotational LB. Not a positional need fill — this is a core special teams retention.

Re-signed March 11. Leonard is a core special teams gunner who also flashed as a CB with an interception in the playoff loss to New England. His 1yr/$2M contract is a minimum-level deal. With St-Juste gone, CB depth is thinner, but Leonard's return adds a little bit back. CB remains a legitimate draft need — this does nothing to address a starting CB need.

Re-signed March 14 on a 1-year deal worth up to $6.75M. Lance provides competent backup QB insurance behind Justin Herbert after playing in 4 games in 2025 including a regular-season-finale start. QB is not a need with Herbert firmly entrenched as the starter. This is smart backup depth at a reasonable cost.
Re-signed as the team's long snapper. Special teams continuity signing — not a positional need impact.

Tendered as an ERFA on March 6, locking him in at league minimum. Vidal produced a career year in 2025 with 643 rushing yards and 3 TDs with three 100-yard games. Pairs with Hampton and Mitchell for a deep backfield. His low cost is effectively free value.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Biadasz (3yr/$30M) fully addresses center. Cole Strange (2yr/$13M) is a legitimate but unproven starter candidate at guard. Both Zion Johnson (Browns, 3yr/$49.5M) and Mekhi Becton (released) are gone, leaving both guard spots open. Strange is the only true IOL addition at guard; his contract signals a competition-for-job, not a lock. Elgton Jenkins (released by Packers) remains available. Guard (especially RG) is still the highest-priority offensive need entering the draft. Tier drops from 1 to 2 only because center is now resolved and Strange provides a baseline.

Mack's return (1yr/$18M, fully guaranteed) is important but he is 35 and on a 1-year bridge deal. Oweh is gone to Washington (4yr/$100M), removing the premier pass rusher. Tuipulotu (13.5 sacks, final contract year) anchors the group but an extension is needed soon. No multi-year EDGE investment was made in FA. EDGE depth is thin — the Chargers need a young EDGE3 via the draft or additional FA. Need tier stays at 2 — not as urgent as IOL, but a premium draft target remains warranted, especially with Tuipulotu's extension looming.

Tart's 3yr/$30M extension locks in the best run-stopper and franchise cornerstone of the DL. Tomlinson adds rotational depth on a 1-year deal. Jamaree Caldwell (3rd-round 2025 pick) and Justin Eboigbe (4th-round 2024) provide young rotational depth. Ogbonnia and Hand departures replaced by Tomlinson. IDL is a solved need for 2026 — at most a late-day-3 depth consideration in the draft.

Kolar's 3yr/$24.3M deal ($17M guaranteed) is a real multi-year investment at a position of need. He pairs with Oronde Gadsden for a legitimate 1A/1B TE room. The contract structure — 3 years with significant guarantees, player aged 27 — signals this is their long-term answer, not a stopgap. TE need is done for 2026 and beyond. At most, a late-round flier for developmental depth.

Hampton (starter, 2025 first-rounder), Mitchell (2yr/$9.25M), and ERFA Vidal form one of the best backfield trios in the AFC. Ingold adds a true fullback for the McDaniel wide-zone system. Najee Harris departure is seamlessly replaced. RB is no longer a need — at most a late-round developmental pick.

St-Juste's departure to Green Bay removes a proven rotational CB. Only Deane Leonard (1yr minimum) was re-signed at CB in FA. Tarheeb Still (starter) and Leonard are the depth pieces behind the top CB spot. No starter-level CB was added in free agency. CB remains a Tier 2 need — a legitimate mid-round draft target. The loss of St-Juste and absence of a CB signing keeps this unchanged.

Rashawn Slater (LT) and Joe Alt (RT) are both expected to return healthy from season-ending injuries. Pipkins (2yr/$10M) and Penning (1yr) provide capable swing tackle depth. OT is not a significant need assuming health returns — the starters are elite when healthy. Depth is adequate. Drop to Tier 4.

See above. Net: Oweh out (massive loss), Mack back (1-year bridge). Net change at the position is negative. Need tier unchanged at 2. Third EDGE rusher is the specific gap — could come via Day 2 draft pick or remaining FA market.

key_additions.headline: Former Pro Bowl center fills biggest pre-FA need, replacing retired Bradley Bozeman with a clear upgrade at C.
Future Hall of Famer returns for Year 5 in LA, anchoring the pass rush after Oweh's departure to Washington.
Multi-year investment at a barren position — Kolar and Gadsden form a legitimate starter TE room for McDaniel's offense.
McDaniel scheme-fit guard reunites with his former head coach to compete for a starting role on a rebuilt interior line.
Chargers lock up their best defensive lineman early, preventing another Poona Ford-type loss at the position.
Explosive 6.3 YPC speedster gives McDaniel's wide-zone offense a legitimate big-play complement to Omarion Hampton.
Pro Bowl fullback reunites with McDaniel, bringing four seasons of system familiarity to power the Chargers' ground game.
Durable 9-year veteran (142 career starts) replaces Hand and Ogbonnia in the DT rotation behind Tart.
2nd-Team All-Pro special teamer (NFL-high 24 ST tackles in 2025) retained to anchor one of the league's best coverage units.
Proven swing tackle depth retained behind Slater and Alt as the Chargers prioritize OL health insurance.

key_losses.headline: Biggest departure of the offseason — Oweh's 7.5-sack, 2-forced-fumble playoff performance earned him a $100M deal LA was unwilling to match.
2022 first-round pick departs to Cleveland, stripping the already-battered interior OL of its lone returning guard.
Cap casualty after one injury-plagued and inconsistent season — his release left the Chargers without a single interior OL starter.
Starting center's February retirement created the most urgent need heading into free agency, quickly addressed by the Biadasz signing.
Breakout rotational CB departs after one excellent year under Jesse Minter, thinning a CB room that already had limited depth.
Impactful rotational DL (1.5 sacks, 1 INT in 2025) lost for just $3M — partially offset by the Tomlinson signing on the same day.
